role_summary: Private secretary at the NAACP national office (1948-1955); contributor to the Brown v. Board of Education legal work.

photo.alt: Cecilia Suyat Marshall (left) with Thurgood Marshall (right) and their sons, August 24, 1965
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division)
photo.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions)

ai_reading: Marshall's top semantic neighbor is Mildred Bond Roxborough (entry 90, count 78), then NAACP LDF lawyer Robert L. Carter (entry 108). The NAACP-front-office cluster matches the cultural placement of Marshall as a 1948-1955 staff member of the national office and a participant in the legal work that became Brown v. Board of Education. What the embedding adds is a clear pull toward Sacred Framing on the Sacred vs Secular axis (position -0.23): for a legal-secretary career anchored in a constitutional-litigation institution, this Sacred-side reading is unexpected and may reflect her oral history's emphasis on family, the church spaces of her Hawaii childhood, and the religious framing of her marriage to Thurgood Marshall at St. Philip's Episcopal Church in Harlem.
biographical_paragraph: Cecilia Suyat Marshall worked the NAACP's national office from 1948 to 1955 as private secretary to Dr. Gloster B. Current, the organization's national director, and on staff there she also took part in the legal work that became Brown v. Board of Education [src: 1]. She was born July 20, 1928 in Pu'unene, on Maui in what was then the Territory of Hawaii; her parents had emigrated from the Philippines in 1910 [src: 1]. In December 1955, the same year she stepped away from Current's office, she married Thurgood Marshall at St. Philip's Episcopal Church in Harlem; the marriage ran until Marshall's death in 1993 [src: 1]. She died on November 22, 2022 in Falls Church, Virginia, at ninety-four [src: 1]. Her oral history is held at the Library of Congress as entry 15 of the Civil Rights History Project [src: 1].
movement_context: The NAACP national office Marshall worked at 20 West Fortieth Street in Manhattan from 1948 to 1955 was the institutional home of the desegregation litigation campaign Thurgood Marshall directed through the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund (LDF), which the organization had formally spun off as a separate 501(c)(3) tax-exempt entity in 1940 to preserve the parent NAACP's lobbying capacity while protecting LDF's litigation budget [src: 2]. Gloster B. Current, the NAACP national director Cecilia Suyat served as private secretary to, ran the field-office network and branch coordination that supplied the local-plaintiff infrastructure the LDF case-strategy needed; the case selection that produced Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954) was the result of two decades of LDF-Current-Marshall coordination through that office [src: 2, 3]. The Brown decision came down May 17, 1954, the year Suyat had been three years on staff; the family photograph used in the hero image of this page (Cecilia, Thurgood, and their sons Thurgood Jr. and John) was taken August 24, 1965 in Washington D.C., the day after Thurgood Marshall was sworn in as the thirty-third Solicitor General of the United States in the Lyndon Johnson administration [src: 4]. Marshall would serve as Solicitor General through August 1967, when Johnson elevated him to the Supreme Court of the United States; Cecilia became the wife of a Supreme Court justice on October 2, 1967 [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: After Thurgood Marshall's death on January 24, 1993, Cecilia became the principal archival caretaker of his papers; the Thurgood Marshall Papers at the Library of Congress Manuscript Division (collection MSS 36486; finding aid published 1996) are the result of her decade-long curation and transfer work, and are the canonical primary-source archive on Marshall's NAACP, Second Circuit, Solicitor General, and Supreme Court years [src: 5]. She continued public-speaking and commemoration work into her nineties, including the May 17, 2014 sixtieth-anniversary Brown commemoration at the U.S. Supreme Court hosted by Chief Justice John Roberts; she was the senior surviving NAACP national-office staff member from the Brown years at the time of her November 22, 2022 death [src: 1]. The 2019 Larry S. Gibson monograph Young Thurgood: The Making of a Supreme Court Justice draws on her recollections in the Maryland Historical Society oral-history record she gave Gibson in the late 2010s [src: 6].

gallery.alt: George E. C. Hayes, Thurgood Marshall, and James Nabrit Jr. celebrating the Brown v. Board of Education victory on the U.S. Supreme Court steps, May 17, 1954
gallery.caption: The three NAACP LDF lawyers principally responsible for Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954), celebrating the unanimous opinion on the U.S. Supreme Court steps on May 17, 1954: from left, George Edward Chalmer Hayes (Howard University Law School, lead counsel in the District of Columbia companion case Bolling v. Sharpe), Thurgood Marshall (LDF director, lead counsel), and James Nabrit Jr. (Howard University Law School). Wire-service photograph from the New York World-Telegram and Sun collection at the Library of Congress.
gallery.photographer: Unknown (New York World-Telegram and Sun staff)
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from Library of Congress, New York World-Telegram and Sun collection)
gallery.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions)

gallery.alt: Betsy Graves Reyneau, Thurgood Marshall (oil on canvas, 1956)
gallery.caption: Betsy Graves Reyneau's 1956 oil-on-canvas portrait of Thurgood Marshall (127 by 81.3 cm), painted two years after the Brown decision and a year after his December 1955 marriage to Cecilia Suyat. The portrait is in the permanent collection of the National Portrait Gallery in Washington, D.C., gifted to the Smithsonian Institution by the Harmon Foundation in January 1967, six months before Marshall's elevation to the Supreme Court.
gallery.photographer: Betsy Graves Reyneau (artist; 1889-1964); image via Google Art Project
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Google Art Project; National Portrait Gallery, Smithsonian Institution)
gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1963 without copyright renewal)

sources.title: Cecilia Suyat Marshall oral history

sources.title: Mark V. Tushnet, Making Civil Rights Law: Thurgood Marshall and the Supreme Court, 1936-1961
sources.publisher: Oxford University Press, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 30113930 (the standard institutional history of the NAACP LDF litigation campaign Marshall directed during the years Cecilia Suyat was on staff at the national office)

sources.title: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)
sources.publisher: U.S. Supreme Court (unanimous opinion of the Court, Warren, C.J.; the foundational school-desegregation precedent the NAACP LDF won during Suyat's tenure at the national office)

sources.title: Juan Williams, Thurgood Marshall: American Revolutionary
sources.publisher: Times Books / Random House, 1998; WorldCat OCLC 38059842 (the canonical Marshall biography drawing on extensive Cecilia Suyat Marshall interviews; documents the August 1965 Solicitor General swearing-in the family hero photograph anticipates)

sources.title: Thurgood Marshall Papers finding aid, Manuscript Division
sources.publisher: Library of Congress, Manuscript Division (collection MSS 36486; finding aid published 1996; Cecilia Suyat Marshall coordinated the deposit and curation of the papers after Justice Marshall's January 24, 1993 death)

sources.title: Larry S. Gibson, Young Thurgood: The Making of a Supreme Court Justice
sources.publisher: Prometheus Books, 2012 (revised 2019); WorldCat OCLC 812250247 (the Maryland Historical Society-anchored biography of Marshall's pre-1954 years, drawing on Cecilia Suyat Marshall oral history)
